volumeTitle: Trojan Women. Iphigenia among the Taurians. Ion
label: Trojan Women (Section 3.660 - Section 865)

sections.label: Section 3.660
sections.original: δάμαρτα · δουλεύσω δʼ ἐν αὐθεντῶν δόμοις .
sections.translation: as his wife , and I must serve in the house of murderers . And if I set aside my love for Hector , and open my heart to this new lord , I shall appear a traitress to the dead , while , if I hate him , I shall incur my master ’ s displeasure .

sections.label: Section 3.665
sections.original: καίτοι λέγουσιν ὡς μίʼ εὐφρόνη χαλᾷ
sections.translation: And yet they say a single night removes a woman ’ s dislike for her husband ; I despise the woman who , when she has lost her former husband , transfers her love by marrying another . Not even the horse , if parted from her stablemate ,

sections.label: Section 3.670
sections.original: τῆς συντραφείσης , ῥᾳδίως ἕλξει ζυγόν .
sections.translation: will cheerfully draw the yoke ; and animals have neither speech nor sense to help them , and are by nature man ’ s inferiors .

sections.label: Section 3.673
sections.original: σὲ δʼ , ὦ φίλʼ Ἕκτορ , εἶχον ἄνδρʼ ἀρκοῦντά μοι
sections.translation: O my dear Hector , in you I found a husband amply dowered with wisdom , noble birth and fortune , a brave man and a mighty ;

sections.label: Section 3.675
sections.original: ἀκήρατον δέ μʼ ἐκ πατρὸς λαβὼν δόμων
sections.translation: while you took from my father ’ s house a spotless bride , yourself the first to make this maiden wife . But now death has claimed you , and I am soon to sail to Hellas , a captive doomed to wear the yoke of slavery . Has not then the dead Polyxena ,

sections.label: Section 3.680
sections.original: Πολυξένης ὄλεθρος , ἣν καταστένεις ;
sections.translation: for whom you wail , less evil to bear than I ? I have not so much as hope , the last resource of every human heart , nor do I beguile myself with dreams of future bliss , the very thought of which is sweet .

sections.label: Section 3.684
sections.original: ἐς ταὐτὸν ἥκεις συμφορᾶς · θρηνοῦσα δὲ
sections.translation: You are in the same plight as I ; your lamentations

sections.label: Section 3.685
sections.original: τὸ σὸν διδάσκεις μʼ ἔνθα πημάτων κυρῶ .
sections.translation: for yourself remind me of my own sad case .

sections.label: Section 3.686
sections.original: αὐτὴ μὲν οὔπω ναὸς εἰσέβην σκάφος ,
sections.translation: I never yet have set foot on a ship ’ s deck , though I have seen such things in pictures and know of them from hearsay . Now sailors , if there comes a storm of moderate force , are all eagerness to save themselves by toil ;

sections.label: Section 3.690
sections.original: ὁ μὲν παρʼ οἴαχʼ , ὁ δʼ ἐπὶ λαίφεσιν βεβώς ,
sections.translation: one stands at the tiller , another sets himself to work the sheets , a third meanwhile is baling out the ship ; but if tempestuous waves arise to overwhelm them , they yield to fortune and commit themselves to the driving billows . Even so I , by reason of my countless troubles ,

sections.label: Section 3.695
sections.original: ἄφθογγός εἰμι καὶ παρεῖσʼ ἐῶ στόμα ·
sections.translation: am speechless and forbear to say a word ; for this surge of misery from the gods is too strong for me . Cease , my darling child , to speak of Hector ’ s fate ; no tears of yours can save him ; honor your present master ,

sections.label: Section 3.700
sections.original: φίλον διδοῦσα δέλεαρ ἀνδρὶ σῶν τρόπων .
sections.translation: offering your sweet nature as the bait to win him . If you do this , you will cheer your friends as well as yourself and you shalt rear my Hector ’ s child to lend stout aid to Ilium , that so your children in the aftertime

sections.label: Section 3.705
sections.original: κατοικίσειαν , καὶ πόλις γένοιτʼ ἔτι .
sections.translation: may build her up again , and our city yet be established . But our talk must take a different turn ; who is this Achaean servant I see coming here again , sent to tell us of some new design ?

sections.label: Section 3.709
sections.original: Φρυγῶν ἀρίστου πρίν ποθʼ Ἕκτορος δάμαρ ,
sections.translation: You that once were the wife of Hector , bravest of the Phrygians ,

sections.label: Section 3.710
sections.original: μή με στυγήσῃς · οὐχ ἑκὼν γὰρ ἀγγελῶ .
sections.translation: do not hate me , for I am not a willing messenger . The Danaids and sons of Pelops both command —

sections.label: Section 3.712
sections.original: τί δʼ ἔστιν ; ὥς μοι φροιμίων ἄρχῃ κακῶν .
sections.translation: What is it ? your prelude bodes evil news .

sections.label: Section 3.713
sections.original: ἔδοξε τόνδε παῖδα ⋯ πῶς εἴπω λόγον ;
sections.translation: It is decreed your son is — how can I tell my news ?

sections.label: Section 3.714
sections.original: μῶν οὐ τὸν αὐτὸν δεσπότην ἡμῖν ἔχειν ;
sections.translation: Surely not to have a different master from me ?

sections.label: Section 3.715
sections.original: οὐδεὶς Ἀχαιῶν τοῦδε δεσπόσει ποτέ .
sections.translation: None of all Achaea ’ s chiefs shall ever lord it over him .

sections.label: Section 3.716
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἐνθάδʼ αὐτοῦ λείψανον Φρυγῶν λιπεῖν ;
sections.translation: Is it their will to leave him here , a remnant of Phrygia ’ s race ?

sections.label: Section 3.717
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδʼ ὅπως σοι ῥᾳδίως εἴπω κακά .
sections.translation: I know no words to break the sorrow lightly to you .

sections.label: Section 3.718
sections.original: ἐπῄνεσʼ αἰδῶ , πλὴν ἐὰν λέγῃς καλά .
sections.translation: I thank you for your consideration , unless indeed you have good news to tell .

sections.label: Section 3.719
sections.original: κτενοῦσι σὸν παῖδʼ , ὡς πύθῃ κακὸν μέγα .
sections.translation: They mean to slay your son ; there is my hateful message to you .

sections.label: Section 3.720
sections.original: οἴμοι , γάμων τόδʼ ὡς κλύω μεῖζον κακόν .
sections.translation: Oh me ! this is worse tidings than my forced marriage .

sections.label: Section 3.721
sections.original: νικᾷ δʼ Ὀδυσσεὺς ἐν Πανέλλησιν λέγων ⋯
sections.translation: So spoke Odysseus to the assembled Hellenes , and his word prevails .

sections.label: Section 3.722
sections.original: αἰαῖ μάλʼ · οὐ γὰρ μέτρια πάσχομεν κακά .
sections.translation: Oh , once again alas ! there is no measure in the woes I bear .

sections.label: Section 3.723
sections.original: λέξας ἀρίστου παῖδα μὴ τρέφειν πατρὸς ⋯
sections.translation: He said they should not rear so brave a father ’ s son .

sections.label: Section 3.724
sections.original: τοιαῦτα νικήσειε τῶν αὑτοῦ πέρι .
sections.translation: May such counsels prevail about children of his !

sections.label: Section 3.725
sections.original: ῥῖψαι δὲ πύργων δεῖν σφε Τρωικῶν ἄπο .
sections.translation: He must be thrown from Troy ’ s battlements . Let it be so , and you will show more wisdom ; do not cling to him , but bear your sorrows with heroic heart , nor in your weakness think that you are strong . For nowhere do you have any help ; consider this you must ;

sections.label: Section 3.730
sections.original: πόλις τʼ ὄλωλε καὶ πόσις , κρατῇ δὲ σύ ,
sections.translation: your husband and your city are no more , so you are in our power , and I alone am match enough for one woman ; therefore I would not see you bent on strife , or any course to bring you shame or hate , nor would I hear you rashly curse the Achaeans .

sections.label: Section 3.735
sections.original: εἰ γάρ τι λέξεις ὧν χολώσεται στρατός ,
sections.translation: For if you say anything to anger the army , this child will find no burial nor pity either . But if you hold your peace and with composure take your fate , you will not leave his corpse unburied , and you yourself will find more favor with the Achaeans .

sections.label: Section 3.740
sections.original: ὦ φίλτατʼ , ὦ περισσὰ τιμηθεὶς τέκνον ,
sections.translation: My dearest ! my own sweet child and priceless treasure ! your death the foe demands , and you must leave your wretched mother . That which saves the lives of others , proves your destruction — your father ’ s nobility ; to you your father ’ s valiancy has proved no gift .

sections.label: Section 3.745
sections.original: ὦ λέκτρα τἀμὰ δυστυχῆ τε καὶ γάμοι ,
sections.translation: O my unlucky bed and marriage , that brought me once to Hector ’ s home , hoping to be the mother of a son that should rule over Asia ’ s fruitful fields instead of serving as a victim to the Danaids ! Do you weep , my child ? do you know your hapless fate ?

sections.label: Section 3.750
sections.original: τί μου δέδραξαι χερσὶ κἀντέχῃ πέπλων ,
sections.translation: Why clutch me with your hands and to my garment cling , nestling like a tender chick beneath my wing ? Hector will not rise from the earth and come gripping his famous spear to bring you salvation ; no kinsman of your father appears , nor might of Phrygian hosts ;

sections.label: Section 3.755
sections.original: λυγρὸν δὲ πήδημʼ ἐς τράχηλον ὑψόθεν
sections.translation: one dreadful headlong leap from the dizzy height and you will dash out your life with none to pity you ! Oh to clasp your tender limbs , a mother ’ s fondest joy ! Oh to breathe your fragrant breath ! In vain it seems these breasts did suckle you , wrapped in your swaddling-clothes ;

sections.label: Section 3.760
sections.original: μάτην δʼ ἐμόχθουν καὶ κατεξάνθην πόνοις .
sections.translation: all for nothing I used to toil and wear myself away ! Kiss your mother now for the last time , nestle to her that bore you , twine your arms about my neck and join your lips to mine ! O you Hellenes , cunning to devise new forms of cruelty ,

sections.label: Section 3.765
sections.original: τί τόνδε παῖδα κτείνετʼ οὐδὲν αἴτιον ;
sections.translation: why slay this child who never wronged any ? You daughter of Tyndareus , you are no child of Zeus , but I say you were born of many a father , first of some evil demon , next of Envy , then of Murder and of Death , and every horror that the earth breeds .

sections.label: Section 3.770
sections.original: οὐ γάρ ποτʼ αὐχῶ Ζῆνά γʼ ἐκφῦσαί σʼ ἐγώ ,
sections.translation: That Zeus was never father of yours I boldly do assert , bane as you have been to many a Hellene and barbarian too . Destruction catch you ! Those fair eyes of yours have brought a shameful ruin on the fields of glorious Troy . Take the child and bear him hence , hurl him down if you wish ,

sections.label: Section 3.775
sections.original: δαίνυσθε τοῦδε σάρκας . ἔκ τε γὰρ θεῶν
sections.translation: then feast upon his flesh ! It is the gods ’ will we perish , and I cannot ward the deadly stroke from my child . Hide me and my misery ; cast me into the ship ’ s hold ; for it is to a fair wedding I am going , now that I have lost my child !

sections.label: Section 3.780
sections.original: τάλαινα Τροία , μυρίους ἀπώλεσας
sections.translation: Unhappy Troy ! you have lost countless men for the sake of one woman and her hateful bed .

sections.label: Section 782
sections.original: ἄγε παῖ , φίλιον πρόσπτυγμα μεθεὶς
sections.translation: Come , child , leave fond embracing of your woeful mother , and mount the high coronal of your ancestral towers ,

sections.label: Section 785
sections.original: πνεῦμα μεθεῖναι ψῆφος ἐκράνθη .
sections.translation: there to draw your parting breath , as is ordained . Take him away . His should the duty be to do such herald ’ s work , whose heart knows no pity and who loves ruthlessness more than my soul does . Exeunt Andromache and Talthybius with Astyanax .

sections.label: Section 790
sections.original: ὦ τέκνον , ὦ παῖ παιδὸς μογεροῦ ,
sections.translation: O child , son of my hapless boy , an unjust fate robs me and your mother of your life . How is it with me ? What can I do for you , luckless one ? For you I strike upon my head and beat my breast , my only gift ;

sections.label: Section 795
sections.original: τῶνδε γὰρ ἄρχομεν . οἲ ʼγὼ πόλεως ,
sections.translation: for that is in my power . Woe for my city ! woe for you ! What sorrow do we not have ? What is wanting now to our utter and immediate ruin ?

sections.label: Section 1.799
sections.original: μελισσοτρόφου Σαλαμῖνος ὦ βασιλεῦ Τελαμών ,
sections.translation: O Telamon , King of Salamis , the feeding-ground of bees ,

sections.label: Section 1.800
sections.original: νάσου περικύμονος οἰκήσας ἕδραν
sections.translation: who have your home in a seagirt isle that lies near the holy hills where first Athena made the grey olive branch to appear , a crown for heavenly heads and a glory to happy Athens , you came , you came in knightly brotherhood

sections.label: Section 1.805
sections.original: στεύων ἅμʼ Ἀλκμήνας γόνῳ
sections.translation: with that great archer , Alcmena ’ s son , to sack our city Ilium , in days gone by , on your advent from Hellas ;

sections.label: Section 1.809
sections.original: ὅθʼ Ἑλλάδος ἄγαγε πρῶτον ἄνθος ἀτυζόμενος
sections.translation: When he led the chosen flower of Hellas , vexed

sections.label: Section 1.810
sections.original: πώλων , Σιμόεντι δʼ ἐπʼ εὐρείτᾳ πλάταν
sections.translation: for the steeds , [*] and at the fair stream of Simois he stayed his sea-borne ship and fastened cables to the stern , and forth from the ship he took the bow his hand could deftly shoot , to be the doom of Laomedon ; and with the ruddy breath of fire

sections.label: Section 1.815
sections.original: ⋯ πυρὸς φοίνικι πνοᾷ καθελὼν
sections.translation: he wasted the masonry squared by Phoebus ’ line and chisel , and sacked the land of Troy ; so twice in two attacks has the blood-stained spear destroyed Dardania ’ s walls .

sections.label: Section 2.820
sections.original: μάταν ἄρʼ , ὦ χρυσέαις ἐν οἰνοχόαις ἁβρὰ βαίνων ,
sections.translation: In vain , it seems , you Phrygian boy [*] pacing with dainty step among your golden chalices , do you fill high the cup of Zeus , a lovely service ;

sections.label: Section 2.825
sections.original: ἁ δέ σε γειναμένα πυρὶ δαίεται ·
sections.translation: the land of your birth is being consumed by fire . The shore re-echoes to our cries ; and , as a bird bewails its young ,

sections.label: Section 2.830
sections.original: ον τεκέων ὕπερ βοᾷ ,
sections.translation: so we bewail our husbands or our children , or our old mothers . The dew-fed springs where you bathed , the course where you trained ,

sections.label: Section 2.835
sections.original: βεβᾶσι , σὺ δὲ πρόσωπα νεα-
sections.translation: are now no more ; but you beside the throne of Zeus are sitting with a calm , sweet smile upon your fair young face , while the spear of Hellas has destroyed the land of Priam .

sections.label: Section 2.840
sections.original: Ἔρως Ἔρως , ὃς τὰ Δαρδάνεια μέλαθρά ποτʼ ἦλθες
sections.translation: Ah ! Love , Love , who once sought these Dardanian halls , deep-seated in the hearts of heavenly gods , how high you made Troy to tower in those days ,

sections.label: Section 2.845
sections.original: κῆδος ἀναψάμενος . τὸ μὲν οὖν Διὸς
sections.translation: allying her with deities ! But I will cease to urge reproaches against Zeus ; for white-winged dawn , whose light is dear to man ,

sections.label: Section 2.850
sections.original: φέγγος ὀλοὸν εἶδε γαῖαν ,
sections.translation: turned a baleful eye upon our land and watched the ruin of our citadel , though she had within her bridal bower a husband to give her children , [*] from this land ,

sections.label: Section 2.855
sections.original: ὃν ἀστέρων τέθριππος ἔλα-
sections.translation: whom once a chariot of gold spangled stars caught up and carried there , great source of hope to his native country ; but all the love the gods once had for Troy is passed away .

sections.label: Section 860
sections.original: ὦ καλλιφεγγὲς ἡλίου σέλας τόδε ,
sections.translation: Hail ! you radiant flare of the sun , by whose fair light I now shall capture her that was my wife , Helen ; for I am that Menelaus , who has toiled so hard , I and Achaea ’ s army . I came to Troy , not so much as men suppose

sections.label: Section 865
sections.original: γυναικὸς οὕνεκʼ , ἀλλʼ ἐπʼ ἄνδρʼ ὃς ἐξ ἐμῶν
sections.translation: for , the sake of a woman , but to punish the man who from my house stole my wife , traitor to my hospitality . But he , by the gods ’ will , has paid the penalty , ruined , and his country too , by the spear of Hellas . And I have come to bear that wretched woman away — wife I have no mind
